entries.title: Daniel Palencia serves Cubs a Classic reminder
entries.summary: <p>MESA, Ariz. — <b>Pete Crow-Armstrong</b>, <b>Alex Bregman</b> and <b>Daniel Palencia</b> are wearing Cubbie blue again. Their Team USA and Venezuela uniforms are in storage now that the World Baseball Classic is over, although Palencia proudly wore a Venezuela jersey walking into the Cubs’ clubhouse Friday after the team’s day off.</p><p>Even though Venezuela defeated the USA in the title game Tuesday, Crow-Armstrong won’t forget the experience anytime soon.</p><p>“That was some of the more fun baseball I’ve ever been a part of,” Crow-Armstrong said. “I really enjoyed playing in front of the different crowds. American fans can take something from the joy and passion, the energy that is put out by these fan bases. It’s a really fun game, and they add to the excitement. I’d do it 10 times out of 10 if asked again.</p><p>“It was a bummer that we came up short. It was a good experience with all those guys, but ultimately it didn’t end the way we wanted.”</p><p>Was Armstrong happy for his teammate Palencia, who nailed down a 3-2 win in his fifth scoreless appearance of the tournament?</p><p>“I love Danny, but we lost, so ... ,” Armstrong said.</p><p>Shortly after Crow-Armstrong said that, Palencia walked past in his Venezuela jersey, making sure Crow-Armstrong saw it.</p><p>“It’s funny now,” Crow-Armstrong said. “It wasn’t funny then.</p><p>“He pitched out of his mind.”</p><p>Manager <b>Craig Counsell</b> said Palencia, who was in midseason form, will probably pitch in one game before camp breaks Tuesday.</p><h3>Dan can do</h3><p>Palencia was so filled with emotions in that moment Tuesday that he had tears in his eyes before he threw the last pitch of the tournament.</p><p>“I was crying. I knew that moment was mine. ‘I can do it, and he’s going to swing and miss,’ ’’ Palencia said. “I just knew it, you know? You have to be romantic with baseball.”</p><p>When <b>Bryce Harper’s</b> home run tied the game, Palencia said he prayed that Venezuela would take the lead so he could be put in the save situation he was dreaming of the night before.</p><p>“I am so happy, I have no words to describe it,” Palencia said.</p><h3>Notes</h3><p>Right fielder <b>Seiya Suzuki </b>(sprained right knee) didn’t play Friday but hoped to add some work to his routine Saturday. A decision on his availability for Opening Day on Thursday is expected in the next day or two. Suzuki could land on the 10-day injured list Sunday but have the stint backdated.</p><p>“We’ll make a decision at some point this weekend, but I think we’re making a short-term decision here,” Counsell said.</p><p>• Left-hander <b>Justin Steele</b> has thrown live batting practice twice in his comeback from elbow surgery. He is expected to join the Cubs in late May or June.</p><p>• <b>Ben Brown</b> had been getting starter’s innings for all of camp, but his one-inning outing Wednesday indicates a bullpen role might be in the works, as opposed to a starter’s role at Triple-A Iowa.</p><p>• Top Cubs prospects playing in the Spring Breakout game Saturday night against the Padres include shortstop <b>Jefferson Rojas</b>, first baseman <b>Jonathan Long</b> and infielder <b>Pedro Ramirez</b>. The Spring Breakout will expand to single-elimination tournaments in the Cactus and Grapefruit Leagues in 2027.</p><p>• Right-handers <b>Ethan Roberts</b> and <b>Gavin Hollowell</b> were optioned to Triple-A Iowa, reducing the spring-training roster to 44 with five days left in camp.<br></p>
entries.author: Daryl Van Schouwen

entries.title: Chance the Rapper prevails in legal battle with ex-manager
entries.summary: <p>The five-year-plus legal battle between Chance The Rapper and his former manager, Pat Corcoran, ended Friday as a Cook County jury found Corcoran had failed to prove he was owed the $3.8 million in unpaid commissions and royalties he sought in his lawsuit against his former friend and business partner. </p><p>"I claim victory in the name of the. Lord," Chance, real name Chancelor Bennett, told the Sun-Times after the verdict was announced at the Daley Center. </p><p>Jurors also ruled in Bennett's countersuit, which was combined with Corcoran's lawsuit for purposes of the trial. When the suit was filed, Chance originally had sought $1 million in damages. Jurors decided Corcoran must pay Bennett $35, and recommended that Corcoran turn over the internet domain name ChanceRaps.com, which Corcoran used to sell Chance the Rapper merchandise.</p><p>Corcoran's team argued it was a split decision, with no substantial damages awarded to either side.</p><p>"We respect the jury's decision, but the message to music managers is clear: Get it in writing," said Jay Scharkey, one of Corcoran's attorneys. "The jury award of $35 speaks to how seriously the jury viewed Chance’s case."</p><p>Jurors deliberated about two hours before reaching their verdict.</p><p>Corcoran declined to comment to the Sun-Times. He had claimed he and the rapper had a “sunset clause” entitling him to three years of earnings after he was terminated in April 2020 amid the fallout from Bennett’s critically panned album “The Big Day.”</p><p>During the trial, jurors also heard details of Bennett’s 2021 countersuit accusing Corcoran of breach of duty. </p><p>Over their eight-year working relationship from 2012 to 2020, Bennett and Corcoran had been the music industry model for a successful independent artist-manager partnership without record label interference.</p><p>But as both parties rested their cases earlier Friday, a different narrative emerged before the packed courtroom.</p><p>Corcoran's legal team described a hardworking manager who deserved to be paid for contributing to the ascent of the Grammy-winning rapper while Bennett’s attorneys tried to portray Corcoran as an opportunistic former employee who was fired for a reason and isn’t owed anything.</p><p>“You’ll hear this is a case about greed and people trying to glom onto someone famous and take from them. But that’s not what this story is. It’s about when someone becomes famous and forgets what it took to get him there,” stated Robert Sweeney, lead attorney for Corcoran.</p><p></p><p>"Chance wanted to be famous and Pat wanted to help,” Sweeney added. “Pat believed in the mission and Pat was going to do whatever it took to make Chance famous.”</p><p>In his closing, Sweeney emphasized his client and Bennett had an agreement entitling Corcoran to 15% of net profits for any of the rapper’s projects he worked on, no matter when the money actually came in – even if that was after Corcoran was fired. Based on that agreement, he asserted Corcoran was entitled not only to the sunset clause revenue, but also to “prior to termination” royalties as well as one-time fees for negotiations he conducted with Live Nation for a now-canceled 2019-2020 tour, a Ben & Jerry’s brand partnership and the 2019 Netflix rap competition series “Rhythm & Flow.”</p><p>“It doesn’t matter when money comes in, it matters if you worked on it,” Sweeney told jurors. “If you worked on it, you get paid. Mr. Corcoran is here because he wants to get paid.”</p><p>Bennett’s legal team fought those assertions, arguing that Bennett and Corcoran never had a written contract. The two former partners famously worked on a 2013 handshake deal and verbal agreement — one Bennett’s team claimed was never questioned until Corcoran was fired.</p><p>“There’s not one single document in the seven years they worked together that shows any evidence of a sunset,” stated Bennett's lead attorney, Precious Jacobs-Perry. Instead, Corcoran’s legal team had relied on “unfounded personal attacks” that tried to paint Bennett as someone “a flake and lazy” while making “The Big Day.”</p><p>Jacobs-Perry also alleged Corcoran was overpaid by $312,300 before his termination.</p><p>“Pat’s case is really about Pat’s greed trying to take something that he does not deserve and that was never agreed to. … Pat has been fully compensated and is entitled to nothing more.”</p><p>Bennett’s attorneys focused on the reasons he countersued, alleging Corcoran mishandled several business deals with merchandise companies and was trying to collect kickbacks to benefit his separate management company, Haight Brand.</p><p>“Chance sued Pat on principle after learning about the things Pat was doing behind his back,” said Jacobs-Perry. “Chance decided to stand up for himself and artists everywhere. He made a choice early in his career to be independent, to own his own music and be free from labels and third parties … and it defined everything that followed.”</p><p></p>
entries.author: Selena Fragassi

entries.title: Park District paints over César Chavez mural as calls grow to rename public buildings in Chicago
entries.summary: <p>A Park District mural featuring César Chavez has been painted over as pressure grows to remove memorials and rename public buildings honoring the late Latino civil rights figure.</p><p>This comes after <a class="Link" href="https://www.nytimes.com/2026/03/18/us/cesar-chavez-sexual-abuse-allegations-ufw.html" target="_blank" ><u>a New York Times investigation published Wednesday</u></a> found that Chavez groomed and sexually assaulted young girls, as well as fellow activist Dolores Huerta, when he led the United Farm Workers in the 1960s and ‘70s.</p><p>Chavez, who died in 1993, has long been <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2026/03/18/allegations-of-abuse-by-cesar-chavez-sadden-shock-chicagos-latino-community" target="_blank" ><u>lionized in Chicago’s Latino and labor strongholds</u></a>. The Latino icon is honored with a school named for him in Back of the Yards, a plaque at the Haymarket Memorial in the West Loop, a post office in Pilsen, and several murals across the city.</p><div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The mural “Libertad” originally featured César Chavez (left) among other historic figures along a wall that lines Barrett Park located at 2022 West Cermak Road, in the Pilsen neighborhood, Monday, Dec. 19, 2022. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>One of those murals, titled “Libertad” – “Freedom” in Spanish – is located on a long wall in Pilsen’s Barrett Park, 2022 W Cermak Rd. It was originally painted in 2008 and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/3/10/23628419/chicago-murals-ruth-garcia-barrett-park-libertad-alondra-sepulveda-cermak-road" target="_blank" ><u>restored in 2023 by artist Ruth Garcia</u></a>.</p><p>On Friday a Sun-Times reporter observed fresh green paint covering a part of the mural where Chavez’s face had previously been before. Other historical figures on the mural including Mexican artist Frida Kahlo, Malcolm X, Rosa Parks, and the Rev. Martin Luther King Jr. remained visible.</p><p>A Park District spokesperson confirmed that they had removed Chavez from the mural, saying in a statement: “The Chicago Park District takes recent allegations of misconduct by Cesar Chavez seriously … and are conducting a district-wide review of any other park features that may honor him. Where appropriate, we will take further action consistent with our values and standards.”</p><p>Garcia could not be immediately reached for comment.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div </div><p>Less than a mile away, inside the Cesar Chavez Post Office, 1859 S Ashland Ave, a large picture of Chavez on a postage stamp adorns the wall above a plaque installed in 2003. </p><p>But on Friday, the future of that memorial was also in doubt, as Rep. Jesús “Chuy” García released the following statement:</p><p>“Considering the horrifying allegations that have come to light, my team and I are beginning the legislative process to rename the César Chavez Post Office in my district. We cannot continue honoring his name, out of respect for those he has harmed, including of course, Dolores Huerta, who I consider my friend.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Myah Goff, 24, stands outside the National Mexican Museum of Art, 1852 W 19th St, in the Pilsen neighborhood on Friday, March 20, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Somer Van Benton/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Goff is glad people are standing up for the survivors, and said this should be a moment to reconsider Chavez’s legacy.</p><p>“For a long time, we had such a positive impression,” Goff said, but “I think we should all just be more cautious of the people that we look up to as our idols.”</p><p>Meanwhile, leaders at a Chicago public school named after Chavez have started a process that could change the school’s name.</p><p>Chicago Public Schools officials say leaders at César E. Chávez Multicultural Academic Center in the Back of the Yards neighborhood have begun getting community feedback. Any recommendation would have to go to the Local School Council and ultimately the school board.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-9f0000" name="image-9f0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/286f8ff/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa6%2F2d%2F7381f2fc4cd1820be39948b58ea1%2Fchavezschool-260321-2.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/069663c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa6%2F2d%2F7381f2fc4cd1820be39948b58ea1%2Fchavezschool-260321-2.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/73b21bb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa6%2F2d%2F7381f2fc4cd1820be39948b58ea1%2Fchavezschool-260321-2.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Cesar E. Chavez Multicultural Academic Center located at 4747 S. Marshfield Ave. in the Back of the Yards neighborhood, Friday, March 20, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>At school pick-up on Friday, near two murals depicting Chavez, parent Lourdes Flores expressed dismay at the accusations against him. “I mean, he was a great leader. But yeah, it was shocking to me,” she said.</p><p>Flores, 31, who has a seven year-old son at the school, is open to a name change. But “I feel like that's something … we should all as a community, discuss,” she said.</p><p>Flores then added, “Check the background of who you want to name the school after, just, you know, in case something like this were to happen [again].”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ef0000" name="image-ef0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/07b4917/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8f%2F40%2F9e69e4b2460b87bc5e973452a962%2Fchavezschool-260321-8.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ac124ea/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8f%2F40%2F9e69e4b2460b87bc5e973452a962%2Fchavezschool-260321-8.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d95c22d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8f%2F40%2F9e69e4b2460b87bc5e973452a962%2Fchavezschool-260321-8.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A mural of Cesar Chavez can be seen an exterior wall of Cesar E. entries.author: Somer Van Benton | WBEZ

entries.title: Moises Ballesteros’ blasts are just the start, says Craig Counsell, who put him on Cubs' roster
entries.summary: <p>MESA, Ariz. — <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a> manager Craig Counsell marveled at the power. There will be more where that came from, he said.</p><p>The topic was left-handed-hitting catcher Moises Ballesteros’ 441- and 423-foot homers against the Angels in a Cactus League game Tuesday at Sloan Park.</p><p>“Moises has shown more of the same of what he did last year in the short stint with us,” Counsell said. “He’s a gifted hitter, a natural hitter. I suggested to hitting coaches they stay away from him.”</p><p>That was said tongue-in-cheek, but there is something to that.</p><p>“I did have a meeting with the hitting coaches, called them all together at some point this spring and they got a little nervous,” Counsell said. “‘You guys should stay away from Ballesteros.’ Joking, you know, but they got the message.”</p><p>Counsell had a meeting with Ballesteros on Friday, assuring the 22-year-old he was on the team. He will be the Cubs’ primary designated hitter to start the season. Counsell “got a tip” that Ballesteros was “unnecessarily nervous” about it, “so I told him immediately before he sat down.”</p><p>“Moises has earned that opportunity,” Counsell said. “When you have a player who has options, you kind of have to beat down the door to get yourself in the lineup. You almost have to do a little more to get that opportunity.”</p><p>A natural hitter with easy power, Ballesteros pounded on the door with those two bombs in consecutive at-bats against the Angels. He was batting .355 with a 1.007 OPS in 31 Cactus League at-bats with two doubles to go with the home runs entering Friday.</p><p>“There are just some hitters … he’s so young and he does certain things and he’s going to improve,” Counsell said. “Someday, Moises will hit a lot of home runs. I don’t think it will be this year, but someday he’ll hit a lot of home runs. When you see balls hit like that, with his hitting gifts, as he gets more experience, learns pitchers and his swing, he’s capable of doing that.”</p><p>Making his debut in 2025, the prized Venezuelan with the 5-8, 215-pound frame batted .298/.394/.474 with two homers in his 22-game major-league debut. He made the playoff roster, going 0-for-3 as a pinch hitter.</p><p>“Right now, I’m working on timing,” Ballesteros told the Sun-Times. “And being selective, taking pitches and getting really good ones to hit. And staying healthy.”</p><p>Counsell and staffers were impressed with how Ballesteros drove pitches hard to all parts of the field last season.</p><p>“The power, the exit velocity indicate that the home runs will come,” Counsell said. “There are a lot of ingredients to like.”</p><p>He’s third on the catcher depth chart behind Miguel Amaya and Carson Kelly, but “it’s been fun watching him catch,” Counsell said. “Moises is a very instinctual baseball player. He understands the game, a product of probably catching a lot. That’s going to be a strength of his.”</p><p>Ballesteros, who is viewed around the league as a contender for National League Rookie of the Year, said he wants to win above anything else.</p><p>“I always say my goal is always to pass my numbers from last year,” Ballesteros said. “But I want to stay here until I win two championships. When I played in the playoffs last year, especially with the fans here … I saw the game differently in that situation.</p><p>“You need to win, and in the playoffs, you play for the team and you play for a winner. You don’t play for yourself. That’s what is really important at this level, and every player in here plays for each other, not for themselves.”</p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" data-module data-column-count=3 data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-hide-descriptions="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-c90005" name="list-c90005"></a><div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div class="PageList-header-title"> <a class="" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" target="{borderColor=#dfdfdf, pageNumber=1, titleUrl=https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs, inverseColors=false, pullQuoteIcon=[{image=null, narrowImage=null, _template=/image/Image.hbs, alt=null}], footerBgColor=#000000, title=Latest on the Cubs, bodyFont=font-3, pageCount=4591, items=[{description=[{items=[Never mind that the organization once known as baseball’s lovable losers took 108 years to win one in 2016, its first since 1908. entries.title: Horoscope for Saturday, March 21, 2026
entries.summary: <h2>Moon alert</h2><p>After 1:45 a.m. Chicago time, there are no restrictions to shopping or important decisions. The moon is in Taurus.</p><h2>Aries (March 21-April 19)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>You have a lot of energy to express yourself today. In particular, discussions about finances, earnings, cash flow and possessions will go well. You have a private agenda that you want to address, and you will do so. (You're strong with the sun in your sign.)</p><h2>Taurus (April 20-May 20)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>This is a great day to set your forces in action. Friends, as well as groups and organizations, might help you or listen to you because your enthusiasm is powerful today. In particular, young people want to hear what you have to say. This is an excellent day to set future goals.</p><h2>Gemini (May 21-June 20)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>This is a popular day! It's also a powerful day for you because your ambition is strong, and your ability to impress bosses and people in authority will be successful because of your charisma and enthusiasm. You have high standards today and will welcome a chance to make the world a better place.</p><h2>Cancer (June 21-July 22)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>This is the classic day to stand on your soapbox and preach your ideas to a crowd. You feel positive, and you want to share your interests and opinions with others. Even if something is a bit risky, your optimism will carry enough energy to make it worth the gamble.</p><h2>Leo (July 23-Aug. 22)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>You're high-vis today. People admire you, and they want to hear what you have to say — especially about financial matters related to shared property, inheritances, wills, debt and taxes. People can see that this is not your first rodeo, and they want your advice.</p><h2>Virgo (Aug. 23-Sept. 22)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Your enthusiasm will allow you to set your troops marching today. Friends, spouses, partners and even members of the general public will be encouraged by your positive energy. Whether it's politics or ideas for a better tomorrow, people will listen to what you have to say.</p><h2>Libra (Sept. 23-Oct. 22)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Work-related travel is likely for many of you today. In any case, you will accomplish a lot because you are bursting with enthusiasm and energy. In addition, funding and practical help from another source will be forthcoming. You have it all.</p><h2>Scorpio (Oct. 23-Nov. 21)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Lately you've been in work mode, getting a lot done. Today, however, you want to play, which means you have lots of energy for sports, social outings, playful activities with kids and anything to do with the arts and the entertainment world. Decide to go along to get along.</p><h2>Sagittarius (Nov. 22-Dec. 21)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Because you have a lot of energy and enthusiasm today to make home improvements as well as settle family issues, use this in the best way possible. You can make things better. entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: White Sox prospect Billy Carlson ready to begin his minor-league career at Single-A
entries.summary: <p>TEMPE, Ariz. — White Sox shortstop prospect <b>Billy Carlson</b> had never played in front of as many people as he did during his first Cactus League game March 6 against the Diamondbacks.</p><p>“Just getting that exposure and feeling that adrenaline was pretty cool,” Carlson said. “The guys were awesome; the big-leaguers did a good job of helping us through it and walking us through it.”</p><p>The Sox hope Carlson, the 10th pick in the 2025 draft, gets used to raucous environments as he travels through the system.</p><p>Carlson is the 73rd-ranked prospect, according to MLB.com. He is one of 14 Sox prospects participating in the Spring Breakout game Saturday. <b>Braden Montgomery</b> (No. 36), <b>Caleb Bonemer</b> (No. 61) and <b>Hagen Smith</b> (No. 72) will join him.</p><p>For Carlson, the game is just another steppingstone as he begins his minor-league career, which the organization hopes will end in the majors. He’s a part of an increasingly deep pool of shortstop prospects — <b>William Bergolla Jr.</b>, <b>Kyle Lodise</b>, Bonemer and Carlson are ranked among the team’s top 11 prospects.</p><p>But the advantage of having many athletic shortstops is that they can move around the field and play different positions.</p><p>“We push each other every day,” Carlson said. “It’s super cool to be a part of a super talented group. I’m excited to compete for a spot.”</p><p>Of course, the Sox have <b>Colson Montgomery</b> at shortstop, and he played defense at a Gold Glove level last season after some skepticism that he’d last at the position. He also has taken some reps at third base this spring.</p><p>Carlson has drawn praise for his exceptional defense as he continues to work on his offense. He’ll start the season at Single-A Kannapolis.</p><p>“We have been working on my setup a little bit,” he said.</p><p>“Just being in a good spot to swing from and stuff like that.”</p><p><b>Roster moves</b></p><p>The Sox released right-handers <b>Lucas Sims</b> and <b>Austin Voth</b>, left-hander <b>Ryan Borucki</b> and infielder <b>LaMonte Wade Jr.</b></p><p>Wade had a productive camp, batting .250/.378/.417 with one home run and six RBI, but the Sox didn’t have room at first base for him. Borucki threw five scoreless innings in five appearances.</p><p>Center fielder <b>Jarred Kelenic</b> and catcher <b>Drew Romo</b> were told they were not making the 26-man roster. They will remain in major-league camp.</p><h3>Angels 4, White Sox 3</h3><p><b>Sam Antonacci </b>played his first game in the outfield this spring on Friday. The Sox lack outfield depth, so Antonacci showing he can handle left field could create an easier path to being called up. He was picked off in the first inning after drawing a walk and singled in the third.</p><p>• Right fielder <b>Tristan Peters</b> went 1-for-3.</p><p>• Opening Day starter <b>Shane Smith</b> allowed four runs, two hits and three walks and struck out five.</p><p><b>On deck: </b>Sox at Reds, 8:05 p.m. Saturday, Goodyear, TBA vs. <b>Andrew Abbott</b>.<br></p>
entries.author: Kyle Williams

entries.title: Blackhawks unsurprisingly routed by league-best Avalanche: 'We didn't have it'
entries.summary: <p>Sometimes the most lopsided NHL matchups on paper end in surprise. It happens more frequently than it seems like it should.</p><p>But sometimes they do go exactly as expected, and the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a>' 4-1 loss Friday against the Avalanche fell in that bucket.</p><p>Every factor was working against the Hawks, who were on the second half of a back-to-back set, starting backup goalie Arvid Soderblom and facing the best team in the league.</p><p>Soderblom was actually stellar, making 45 saves, but his hard work was pointless in the face of such a relentless Colorado attack on a young and fatigued Hawks defense.</p><p>"We didn't have it tonight, and they did," coach Jeff Blashill said. "[The Avalanche] snapped the puck around well. They were skating. We weren't skating. We had too many times where we were standing still, with or without the puck, and then you're in a grind."</p><p>The Avalanche finished with absurd advantages in every statistical category: 87-42 in shot attempts, 49-20 in shots on goal and 49-21 in scoring chances.</p><p>A whopping 44 of those Avalanche scoring chances came during five-on-five play — the most the Hawks have allowed in a game since March 2019. But the Hawks' penalty kill, which hasn't been nearly as stout since several of its stalwarts were traded, also allowed two goals.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Jeff Blashill on how Nick Lardis fared on the second line tonight:<br><br>"He was kind of like the rest of our team, not skating enough and not creating enough. But it was our whole team, so I certainly can’t pin that on him."<br><br>"As a group, I didn’t think we were winning enough puck…</p>— Ben Pope (@BenPopeCST) <a href="https://twitter.com/BenPopeCST/status/2035202102536904809?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 21, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p></p><h3><b>Lottery implications</b></h3><p>The NHL draft lottery will take place May 5, but yet to be determined is each team's odds of landing the No. 1 or No. 2 pick headed in.</p><p>The Hawks sit third-to-last in the NHL standings, ahead of only the Canucks and Flames. If they finish there, they would have a 19% chance to get one of those two picks.</p><p>It's actually remarkable that they're in roughly the same lottery position as last season despite having already exceeded last season's point total (64 points vs. 61). That fact reflects of the league's increased parity.</p><p>Controversial Canadian forward Gavin McKenna and less controversial Swedish forward Ivar Stenberg stand out as the consensus top 2026 draft prospects at the moment.</p><p>After them, there's a large group of acclaimed defensemen (including Keaton Verhoeff, Chase Reid, Alberts Smits, Carson Carels and Daxon Rudolph), as well as a few forwards (Caleb Malhotra, Tynan Lawrence, Viggo Bjorck and Ethan Belchetz) whose spots in the hierarchy varies more from ranking to ranking.</p><h3><b>Frondell watch</b></h3><p>While the Hawks continue to wait for forward prospect Sacha Boisvert's U.S. work visa to get approved — that process has been especially slow lately around the league — Frondell may or may not be just days away from also joining the team.</p><p>Frondell's Swedish club, Djurgardens, will play a do-or-die playoff game Saturday against Malmo. If they lose, their season ends. If they win, they advance to a lengthy best-of-seven series in the next round.</p><p>In Djurgardens' regular-season finale last week, Frondell's 20th goal of the season (in 43 games) made him the first 18-year-old to reach that mark in the Swedish league since Daniel Sedin in 1999. That's impressive company.</p><h3><b>Notes</b></h3><p>Louis Crevier and Andrew Mangiapane both missed Friday's game because of undisclosed injuries suffered Thursday in Minnesota. Blashill implied Crevier is day-to-day whereas Mangiapane might be out a little bit longer.</p><p>Forward prospect Gavin Hayes will miss six-to-eight weeks with an MCL injury, meaning his second season in Rockford is likely over. He took a significant step forward in it, recording 25 points during his last 46 games, and he has a chance to become vaguely NHL-relevant next season.</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Transportation department's changes to federal diversity program risks hurting Chicago's small businesses
entries.summary: <p>Some diversity and construction industry advocates say a recent change by the U.S. Department of Transportation to a federal program could hurt smaller companies owned by minorities and women — and possibly drive some in Chicago out of business.</p><p>In October, the federal agency said businesses under the Disadvantaged Business Enterprise program must be recertified without using race or gender as a factor. The directive came amid recent efforts by President Donald Trump’s administration to end diversity initiatives.</p><p>“The American people don’t care what race or gender construction workers, pipefitters or electricians are. They just want these massive projects finally built quickly and efficiently,” the DOT said.</p><p>Congress created the Disadvantaged Business Enterprise program in 1983. It calls for transportation agencies to set contract goals for federally funded infrastructure projects to include smaller “disadvantaged” businesses, typically owned by racial minorities and women.</p><p>Last year, the program included about 50,000 businesses nationwide, including thousands in Illinois.</p><p>The DOT has called the Chicago Transit Authority’s diversity programs “discriminatory” and has said it's reviewing the $5.7 billion Red Line Extension and Red and Purple Modernization projects “to determine whether any unconstitutional practices are occurring.” It froze the remaining federal funding for both projects, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/donald-trump/2025/10/03/donald-trump-pause-funding-chicago-infrastructure-projects-red-line-extension" target="_blank" >totaling $2.1 billion</a>. It also put under review New York’s Second Avenue Subway and Hudson Tunnel projects.</p><p>“Illinois, like New York, is well known to promote race- and sex-based contracting and other racial preferences as a public policy,” the DOT said.</p><p>Illinois lawmakers, including U.S. Sens. Tammy Duckworth and Dick Durbin, sent a <a class="Link" href="https://www.duckworth.senate.gov/news/press-releases/-duckworth-durbin-quigley-kelly-davis-demand-answers-about-frozen-federal-funding-for-chicagos-red-line-extension-project-and-red-and-purple-modernization-project" target="_blank" >letter</a> to the DOT in October, saying pausing federal funding for the CTA’s projects “creates funding uncertainty at the state and local level. It also threatens jobs, economic development and the livelihood of hardworking Americans who rely on public transit every day.”</p><p>On Friday, the CTA <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2026/03/20/cta-red-line-extension-lawsuit" >filed a lawsuit</a> against the federal government over the funding freeze and said if the funds aren't restored the agency would be forced to stop work on both projects.</p><h3>‘Devastating blow’</h3><p>Weakening the DBE program is a “devastating blow” that could put minority-owned construction companies out of business, according to Michael Bempah, chief executive officer of Pinpoint Precision Engineering in Chicago. It would also mean “reduced access for new businesses trying to get started,” he said.</p><p>Bempah’s company has about 25 employees and recently worked on the Kennedy Expressway 90/94 deck repairs.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ba0000" name="image-ba0000"></a> <picture in Oak Brook.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Mark Black/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The new federal policy creates significant uncertainty for infrastructure projects, as well as construction service firms.</p><p>“That has translated into real-world impacts for companies, including delays to pending and upcoming projects as agencies seek clarity and compliance,” said Kevin Artl, chief executive officer of the American Council of Engineering Companies of Illinois.</p><p>The new regulations have had “a disruptive effect across the industry, extending beyond DBE firms to any company working with agencies subject to the new rules,” Artl said.</p><p>According to Bempah, “Some business owners are selling their companies, while others may continue to fight. As for my company, we see this as an opportunity to fight for greater access.”</p><h3>Setback for Illinois?</h3><p>Illinois is considered a leader in opening infrastructure contracts to smaller businesses owned by minorities and women.</p><p>Transportation agencies such as the CTA have some of the most successful DBE programs in the nation, according to Colette Holt of Colette Holt & Associates in Texas, who has studied DBE programs at large infrastructure projects in Illinois and elsewhere.</p><p>The CTA exceeded its goal of awarding <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/small-business/2024/03/09/cta-dbe-program-small-business-construction-red-purple-modernization" >20% of contracts to DBEs</a> in its $2.1 billion renovation of the Red and Purple L lines. The Red and Purple Modernization is the agency’s second-largest project. Minorities accounted for nearly 53% of the project’s workforce, according to the CTA.</p><p>The overhaul of the DBE program “will no doubt make it more challenging to maintain the gains and continue to support the health and growth of minority- and woman-owned firms,” Holt said.</p><p>Adrian Mobley, who owns A&W Contractors in Englewood, made a career shift into construction, spurred by the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/9/26/23890961/cta-red-purple-modernization-project" >Red and Purple Modernization’s</a> goals to include more DBE subcontractors. For 25 years, she was a respiratory therapist at Chicago hospitals and started her own business in 2014 to offer workplace CPR and safety training. She shifted into construction in 2020 and won a $4.5 million subcontract to manage traffic flow and safety at CTA construction sites for the Red and Purple Modernization.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Adrian Mobley, owner of A&W Contractors, at her company’s Englewood office.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Since then, her company has grown from three employees to about 30. Many are union members making nearly $50 an hour.</p><p>Last year, Mobley applied for <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/small-business/2026/02/14/aw-contractors-mobley-federal-diversity-guidelines-dbe" target="_blank" >DBE recertification</a> with the city of Chicago, although the criteria weren't clear. Her five-page application answered open-ended questions seeking a “personal narrative” to prove her business is disadvantaged, she said. Certification still requires financial documentation.</p><p>In January, the city notified her that A&W will be recertified as a DBE. That was “absolutely imperative,” Mobley said. At least 40% of her company’s contracts are for federally funded projects.</p><p>Bempah applied for DBE recertification in November with the Illinois Department of Transportation. The state notified him this month that his company has been recertified.</p><p>“The certification is important because, without it, it limits the amount of networking and subcontracting opportunities for big projects,” Bempah said.</p><p>Without broader diversity goals, big construction companies might not see a need to hire small, local subcontractors.</p><p>“Contract goals work," Holt said. "Agencies that have seen their programs shut down, like Cook County in the early 2000s, saw minority business enterprise participation drastically decline. I hope that doesn’t happen here.”</p><h3>IDOT: DBE goals intact</h3><p>Businesses can be certified as DBEs by different agencies, such as IDOT. Local agencies review applications and grant certification, but their decisions can be audited by the federal government. Deadlines vary and response times var, which can create uncertainty for companies and infrastructure projects.</p><p>IDOT, the CTA and industry organizations such as the American Council of Engineering Companies of Illinois have hosted workshops on the new DBE rules.</p><p>“IDOT will not be including DBE goals on newly advertised projects and contracts until the DBE reevaluation process is completed,” the department announced in November. “Once that reevaluation process is completed, IDOT intends to include DBE goals on projects and contracts where applicable.”</p><p>The Illinois agency said DBE goals will remain intact on contracts made before Oct. 3, 2025, the date of the federal order. It urged the construction industry and partners to continue supporting and hiring DBE firms.</p><p>IDOT must comply with federal regulations, but it says that compliance “does not reflect a change in IDOT’s long-standing commitment to equity, inclusion and opportunity in the transportation industry, especially because all businesses are impacted when there are limited opportunities for small business inclusion.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2026/03/20/cta-red-line-extension-lawsuit" >CTA sues feds over withholding $2B for Red Line extension, warns projects may grind to a halt</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/donald-trump/2025/10/03/donald-trump-pause-funding-chicago-infrastructure-projects-red-line-extension" >Trump freezes $2.1B for Chicago projects, Red Line extension in shutdown fight with Dems</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/small-business/2026/02/14/aw-contractors-mobley-federal-diversity-guidelines-dbe" >A&W Contractors navigates changes in federal diversity guidelines</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Amy Yee

entries.title: Dear Abby: Mom unsure what to do when disturbing truth is revealed
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> My daughter "Kate" is 22 and just recently graduated from college. She has been dating her boyfriend for five years. I love this young man like he was my own.</p><p>The problem is, I just found out Kate has been seeing someone on the side. He is her boss, and he's 54 years old! I'm sworn to secrecy because her older sister told me about this. To say the least, I am disgusted, but I can't say anything because it would destroy the trust between the two sisters. Any advice? — <i>DISGUSTED MOM IN THE EAST</i></p><p><b>DEAR MOM:</b> Yes. My advice is to step back and let the situation evolve. The truth will eventually come out. Either the boss' wife will find out and Kate will have to find another job, or her boyfriend will catch on and end their relationship so he can find a woman who is capable of being faithful. Sometimes people have to learn these life lessons the hard way, and Kate is no exception.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> Our adult grandson is planning a guys trip with three of his friends. We know only one of them well. They'll be visiting us at our lake home and doing all the lake activities. My husband will drive the boat as none of them has any experience.</p><p>I want to tell them I will turn the kitchen over to them and ask them to bring their own groceries and beverages. My husband thinks I should provide the groceries and meals and have them bring their own drinks and help with cleaning up. This has caused a lot of arguments between us. How should we resolve this issue? — <i>FRUSTRATED GRANDMA</i></p><p><b>DEAR GRANDMA:</b> Here's how: Tell your husband you do not plan on shouldering the entire responsibility of feeding five adult males for the duration of their stay. Offer to cook a "welcome dinner" for them, but if he feels they need more hospitality, HE should prepare the rest.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I frequent a small local restaurant. The owner is also the server. I believe her husband (or son) is the chef. We're on friendly terms, and I sometimes get a "free" dessert. When the bill arrives, I am asked on the receipt for a tip, which I have been leaving. But, lately, I feel I'm paying extra for my meal or paying for that "free" dessert — and then some. I mean, who am I tipping? It's not like the server is a hired employee. I need to know, must I leave a tip? What is proper when it comes to tipping the owner who is also the server? — <i>CONFOUNDED IN FLORIDA</i></p><p><b>DEAR CONFOUNDED:</b> You state that this is a "small" local restaurant. Restaurants operate on a narrow margin. If the welcome is warm and you enjoy the food and atmosphere, you shouldn't quibble about rewarding the owner(s) with a tip. I don't think the message would be on the bill if a tip was not expected. The amount is up to you.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>Good advice for everyone — teens to seniors — is in "The Anger in All of Us and How to Deal With It." To order, send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds), to: Dear Abby, Anger Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: How we photographed first responders honoring fallen firefighter Michael Altman
entries.summary: <p>This week’s top photo comes from Assistant Photo Editor Pat Nabong. Follow her on <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/patnabong" target="_blank" >Instagram</a>.</p><h3>What makes this photo important?</h3><p>While many people were tuned into the elections on Tuesday, dozens of Chicago Fire Department firefighters, EMTs and other personnel were saluting the ambulance carrying firefighter Michael Altman's body to the Cook County Medical Examiner's office. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/17/firefighter-seriously-hurt-battling-rogers-park-fire-has-died" >Altman, who came from three generations of firefighters, died Tuesday</a> after he was injured battling <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/16/extra-alarm-blaze-erupts-at-a-rogers-park-building-possibly-injuring-firefighter" >a fire Monday in Rogers Park</a>. </p><p>The news cycle is often relentless, and it's especially true on election days. Sometimes, local stories like a fallen first responder are overshadowed, but people like Altman are part of Chicago's history, and their stories matter.</p><p></p><h3>How did you get the photo?</h3><p>When I arrived at the scene, television cameras were initially positioned a few meters away from the firefighters who were holding the flag. I wanted to keep a respectful distance, so I only moved up when a firefighter told a camera operator that we could get closer. Altman's colleagues began forming a line on each side of the street and they saluted when the procession of fire trucks and the ambulance got closer. I crouched so I could see under the giant flag and through the space between the firefighters who were holding it. The wide photo showed a community of first responders coming together for Altman.</p><p></p><h3>Technical details:</h3><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-c99fb892-fdf8-11f0-8afa-b3d074ccce9f" entries.author: Pat Nabong

entries.title: AIPAC has become a political liability for many seeking office
entries.summary: <p>Two years ago, the brand awareness of AIPAC mostly swirled among political insiders. Today, AIPAC is an increasingly radioactive word and political liability.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://evanstonroundtable.com/2026/03/18/mayor-biss-wins-democratic-nomination-for-9th-congressional-district-seat/" target="_blank" >American Israel Public Affairs Committee</a> is a powerful bipartisan, unapologetic pro-Israel lobby. The <a class="Link" href="https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cde3eyzdr63o" target="_blank" >genocide</a> in Gaza awakened the general public to the outsized influence AIPAC exerts on members of Congress to unconditionally fund Israel. In Tuesday’s Illinois primary, my colleagues reported AIPAC spent close to <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/reels/DV1wSpgETxJ/" target="_blank" >$25 million on four Democratic U.S. House races.</a></p><p>Dark money is hardly a new phenomenon, but this election cycle opened up a number of seats — shaking the political landscape for years to come. <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/politics/2026/02/27/aipac-pro-israel-groups-chicago-area-democratic-congressional-primaries-miller-conyears-ervin-bean-fine" target="_blank" >Through political action committees</a>, money supports campaigns without working directly with candidates. AIPAC money backed winners Cook County Commissioner Donna Miller in the 2nd District and former U.S. Rep. Melissa Bean in the 8th District. PACS working in the 9th and 7th districts didn’t see their preferred candidates win; Evanston Mayor <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/03/17/us-house-illinois-9th-congressional-district-primary-results" >Daniel Biss</a> and state Rep. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/03/17/us-house-illinois-7th-congressional-district-primary-results" >La Shawn Ford</a>, D-Chicago, won in two crowded races.</p><p>"AIPAC is trying to claim victory in the wake of an election in which it refused to be truthful about its own role in that election. They [AIPAC officials] are trying to convince the country that they are a powerful political entity, and yet they didn't have the backbone to tell voters what they were doing while they were doing it," said political strategist Jake Lewis. "Their brand is toxic."</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-460000" name="module-460000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnist </div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Columnist</div> </div><p>The clandestine nature of AIPAC ads and mailers didn’t mention Israel. In fact, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/government-politics/elections/2026/03/18/illinois-primary-super-pac-spending-aipac-cryptocurrency-ai-sports-betting" target="_blank" >its PACS operated under progressive names to lure (or some say confuse) voters.</a> Affordable Chicago Now supported Miller. Chicago Progressive Partnership worked against Kat Abughazaleh in the 9th District, while Elect Democratic Women Action Fund favored state Sen. Laura Fine, D-Glenview, in that race. United Democracy Project poured millions into backing Chicago City Treasurer Melissa Conyears-Ervin in the 7th.</p><p>Political strategist <a class="Link" href="https://joannaklonsky.com/" target="_blank" >Joanna Klonsky</a> said AIPAC’s policy agenda is "extremely unpopular, which is why they had to conceal their identities. Democrats do not want this war in Iran, and they don't want to pay for bombs to drop on Gaza. They certainly don't want to pay for any at the expense of their own health care or other public programs."</p><p>Lewis and Klonsky advised the campaigns of state Sen. Robert Peters, D-Chicago, who lost in the 2nd, and Biss, who on election night triumphantly declared, <a class="Link" href="https://evanstonroundtable.com/2026/03/18/mayor-biss-wins-democratic-nomination-for-9th-congressional-district-seat/" target="_blank" >“AIPAC found out the hard way: the 9th District is not for sale. May tonight be the last time I utter their name.</a>"</p><p>Public awareness about AIPAC has grown over the last several years, and political candidates either embrace or denounce the group with consequences unimaginable even five years ago. The political calculation boils down to: Is taking big dollars a boost or a burden that will alienate some voters? But in the heavily Black 2nd District, where Miller bested Jesse Jackson Jr., AIPAC played less of a factor in the campaign.</p><p>"Communities with privilege have more opportunities to care about AIPAC than necessarily our community. The Black community should look at it a little bit more, but at the same time, our needs for our community are so urgent and in our face that AIPAC does not come to the priority list for why we're voting. I think you'll find that our community does not support a genocide and Gaza, but you'll also find it's not the motivating factor for our voting," said political strategist <a class="Link" href="https://www.apsandassociates.com/about" target="_blank" >Alex Sims-Jones</a>.</p><p>AIPAC lobbyists court potential allies in part by sponsoring all-expense-paid trips to Israel for elected officials and their aides. Over the years, observers say AIPAC has skewed toward the right and the election results in <a class="Link" href="https://truthout.org/articles/is-the-tide-starting-to-turn-against-aipac/" target="_blank" >Illinois and elsewhere demonstrate weakened standing</a>. A recent Quinnipiac University poll found <a class="Link" href="https://poll.qu.edu/poll-release?releaseid=3952" target="_blank" >44% of voters think the U.S. is too supportive of Israel </a>— the highest percentage since the poll started asking in 2017.</p><p>Sabha Abour, another Chicago-based political strategist, said the Democratic Party is fragmented on Israel. So are Republicans.</p><p>"We see that in conservatives now, too, where you have pro-AIPAC conservatives and anti- AIPAC conservatives, and it's not based off of antisemitism. It's based off of the fact that people don't want a foreign country to control their government," Abour said. "There are some candidates who were anti-AIPAC who did lose, but the amount of money that was spent in these races and the amount of people that push the conversation to the left is a win for me."</p><p><i>Natalie Y. Moore is a senior lecturer at Northwestern University.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-420000" name="module-420000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Natalie Y. Moore

entries.title: Chicago seeks to make the West Side's Madison Street shine again
entries.summary: <p>Madison Street on the city's West Side has been rocked by some serious body blows over the last 60 or so years and it shows: the unhealed scars left by the civil unrest that followed the Rev. Martin Luther King Jr.'s 1968 assassination, nearly three generations of disinvestment and scores of vacant commercial buildings.</p><p>Yet the street goes on. Down, perhaps. But never quite out.</p><p>And now, the thoroughfare is the focus of a city study aimed at helping bring new retail, housing and other activity to three miles of Madison Street, stretching from the shadow of the United Center to the heart of K-Town.</p><p>"Madison [is] probably the most visible and historically significant commercial corridor on the West Side," Chicago Department of Planning Supervising Planner Brian Hacker said of the <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicago.gov/content/dam/city/sites/madison-corridor-study/pdfs/Presentation_092925.pdf" target="_blank" >Madison Street Corridor Study</a>. "We're looking at the levers that we can pull as a city planning department — zoning, regulatory, environmental ... to facilitate development."</p><p>It's not a bad time to rethink Madison Street, particularly within the study's boundaries that include the Near West Side, East Garfield Park and West Garfield Park.</p><p>East of the study area, construction will soon begin on the <a class="Link" href="https://www.the1901projectchicago.com/" target="_blank" >1901 Project</a>, a $7 billion effort by the Reinsdorf and Wirtz families to turn those barren parking lots around the United Center, 1901 W. Madison St., into a new neighborhood and entertainment district.</p><p>Also within the study's boundaries are the new $50 million Sankofa Wellness Center, at 4305 W. Madison St., and the planned $42.2 million Madison Street Athletic & Cultural Complex — a project selected by the city in January to be built on a 1-acre city-owned site at 2905-29 W. Madison St.</p><p>The street's existing bones are battered, but they're still good.</p><p>Madison Street runs through the south portion of the remarkable 173-acre William Le Baron Jenney and Jens Jensen-designed Garfield Park. And there is an enviable collection of surviving retail buildings still hanging on at Madison Street and Pulaski Road.</p><p>Not to mention Out of the Past Records, a vinyl records haven at 4407 W. Madison St. that's been an institution in the community since 1968.</p><p>Sharif Walker, CEO of the West Side nonprofit Bethel New Life, is among the 18 community members working with the city on the corridor plan.</p><p>"You have everything you need," Walker said of Madison Street. "You have a major park. You have schools. You have shopping ... all the ingredients are there."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink"
